stem: What differentiates NSTEMI from unstable angina?

choices.feedback: Both conditions involve CAD with plaque instability.
ST elevation defines STEMI. Both NSTEMI and UA can show ST depression or T-wave changes.
Correct! Troponin elevation = myocardial necrosis = NSTEMI. UA has similar symptoms/EKG but negative troponin (no cell death). High-sensitivity troponin detectable within 1-3 hours.
Both UA and NSTEMI can present with chest pain at rest.

explanation: NSTEMI and unstable angina both represent non-ST-elevation acute coronary syndromes caused by partial coronary artery occlusion. The critical distinction is biomarker evidence of myocardial necrosis: NSTEMI shows elevated cardiac troponin (indicating myocyte death), while unstable angina has normal troponins (ischemia without infarction). Both may present identically with chest pain and ECG changes (ST depression, T-wave inversion), so serial troponin measurements at 0 and 3-6 hours are essential. High-sensitivity troponin assays have improved early detection but also require clinical correlation to avoid false positives from non-ACS causes (PE, myocarditis, renal failure).
references: Basit H, Malik A, Huecker MR. Non-ST-Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction. [Updated 2023 Jul 10]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK513228/ [Open Access]

stem: Door-to-balloon time goal for STEMI?

choices.feedback: Correct! PCI door-to-balloon ≤90 min (≤120 min if transfer to PCI-capable facility). If PCI not available within 120 min, give fibrinolytics (door-to-needle ≤30 min). Every minute of delay increases mortality.
Far too long. Rapid reperfusion is critical.
30 min is the door-to-needle goal for fibrinolytics.
While benefit exists up to 12h, the target is ≤90 min.

explanation: The AHA/ACC STEMI guidelines recommend a door-to-balloon time of ≤90 minutes for primary PCI at PCI-capable hospitals. This benchmark reflects the time-dependent nature of myocardial salvage: every 30-minute delay in reperfusion increases 1-year mortality. If PCI cannot be performed within 120 minutes of first medical contact, fibrinolytic therapy should be administered within 30 minutes (door-to-needle time). The total ischemic time goal (symptom onset to reperfusion) should be <120 minutes per 2023 ESC guidelines. These time metrics are core quality measures tracked by the AHA Mission: Lifeline program.
references: Singh A, Museedi AS, Grossman SA. Acute Coronary Syndrome. [Updated 2023 Jul 10]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK459157/ [Open Access]

stem: Inferior STEMI with hypotension, clear lungs, and JVD. What should be AVOIDED?

choices.feedback: IV fluids are the CORRECT treatment — RV infarct is preload-dependent.

Correct! RV infarct = preload-dependent. Nitroglycerin causes venodilation → decreased preload → hemodynamic collapse. Also avoid morphine and diuretics. Treatment: IV fluids to maintain RV preload. Recognize by: inferior STEMI + hypotension + clear lungs + JVD. Confirm with right-sided EKG (ST elevation V4R).
Anticoagulation is still indicated in ACS with RV infarct.
Aspirin should be given to all ACS patients regardless of RV involvement.

explanation: This triad — inferior STEMI, hypotension, clear lungs, and elevated JVP — indicates right ventricular infarction, which complicates approximately 30-50% of inferior STEMIs (the RCA supplies both the inferior LV wall and RV). The RV becomes preload-dependent because it cannot generate adequate forward flow. Nitroglycerin (and other vasodilators/diuretics) reduce preload, which can precipitate catastrophic hemodynamic collapse in RV infarction. Management requires aggressive IV fluid resuscitation to maintain RV preload. Right-sided ECG leads (V4R showing ST elevation) confirm RV involvement.
references: Singh A, Museedi AS, Grossman SA. Acute Coronary Syndrome. [Updated 2023 Jul 10]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK459157/ [Open Access]

stem: Most IMPORTANT initial medication in ACS?

choices.feedback: P2Y12 inhibitors are important adjuncts but aspirin is the first-line priority.

Correct! Aspirin is the single most important initial therapy. Chewing provides faster buccal absorption than swallowing. Irreversibly inhibits platelet COX-1, blocking thromboxane A2 synthesis and platelet aggregation.
Nitroglycerin relieves ischemic symptoms but doesn't reduce mortality like aspirin.
Morphine is used cautiously for refractory pain only. Evidence suggests it may worsen outcomes.

explanation: Aspirin (non-enteric coated, chewed for rapid buccal absorption) is the single most important initial medication in ACS. It irreversibly inhibits cyclooxygenase-1 (COX-1) in platelets, blocking thromboxane A2 production and reducing platelet aggregation at the site of plaque rupture. Every minute of delay in aspirin administration increases mortality. It should be given even before the ECG is obtained if ACS is suspected. Contraindications: true aspirin allergy or active GI hemorrhage.
references: Basit H, Malik A, Huecker MR. Non-ST-Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction. [Updated 2023 Jul 10]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK513228/ [Open Access]

stem: New harsh holosystolic murmur with thrill on day 4 post-MI. Diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Correct! Post-MI VSD presents day 3-5 with a new harsh holosystolic murmur at the left sternal border WITH a palpable thrill (key distinguishing feature from MR). Causes acute left-to-right shunt → heart failure. Diagnosis: echocardiogram shows septal defect with color flow. Treatment: emergent surgical repair.
AS causes a systolic crescendo-decrescendo murmur, not a new holosystolic murmur post-MI.
Dressler = pericarditis (friction rub, fever) occurring weeks post-MI, not a murmur with thrill.
Papillary muscle rupture causes acute MR (holosystolic murmur at apex radiating to axilla) but typically without a palpable thrill.

explanation: A new holosystolic murmur with a palpable thrill occurring 3-5 days after MI is the classic presentation of ventricular septal rupture (VSR). Coagulation necrosis thins and weakens the infarcted septum, which then ruptures under ventricular pressure, creating a left-to-right shunt. The thrill at the left sternal border distinguishes VSR from papillary muscle rupture (PMR), which also presents at 3-5 days post-MI with a holosystolic murmur but at the apex radiating to the axilla, typically without a thrill, and with acute pulmonary edema from severe mitral regurgitation. Diagnosis is confirmed by echocardiography showing the septal defect with left-to-right flow, or by right heart catheterization demonstrating an oxygen saturation step-up in the RV. VSR carries 40-80% mortality without surgical intervention.
references: Mubarik A, Iqbal AM. Ventricular Septal Rupture. [Updated 2022 Sep 19]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK534857/ [Open Access]

stem: Anterior wall STEMI involves which coronary artery?

choices.feedback: LCx supplies the lateral wall (leads I, aVL, V5-V6).
Left main occlusion is usually rapidly fatal or presents as massive anterior + lateral STEMI.
RCA supplies the inferior wall (leads II, III, aVF) in 85% of patients.

Correct! LAD supplies the anterior wall. Anterior STEMI (leads V1-V4) = LAD occlusion. The LAD is called the 'widow-maker' because anterior MI carries the highest mortality and risk of cardiogenic shock/LV dysfunction.

explanation: The LAD supplies the anterior wall of the left ventricle, the anterior two-thirds of the interventricular septum, and the apex. LAD occlusion causes anterior STEMI with ST elevation in leads V1-V4 (extending to V5-V6 with proximal LAD lesions). Other coronary territory correlations: the right coronary artery (RCA) supplies the inferior wall (leads II, III, aVF), and the left circumflex (LCx) supplies the lateral wall (leads I, aVL, V5-V6).
references: Akbar H, Mountfort S. Acute ST-Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI). [Updated 2024 Oct 6]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK532281/ [Open Access]

stem: Fibrinolytics are CONTRAINDICATED in which situation?

choices.feedback: Prior PCI is not a contraindication to fibrinolytics.

Correct! Absolute contraindications to fibrinolytics: aortic dissection (catastrophic hemorrhage), active internal bleeding, prior intracranial hemorrhage, ischemic stroke <3 months, brain tumor, significant head trauma <3 months. Aortic dissection can mimic STEMI (inferior MI from RCA involvement) — always consider before giving lytics.
This is an indication, not a contraindication.
Advanced age increases bleeding risk but is not an absolute contraindication.

explanation: Suspected aortic dissection is an absolute contraindication to fibrinolytic therapy because thrombolysis would dissolve the clot sealing the intimal tear, causing catastrophic hemorrhage into the false lumen and likely fatal rupture. Other absolute contraindications include: active internal bleeding (excluding menses), prior intracranial hemorrhage, ischemic stroke within 3 months, and known intracranial neoplasm. Prior PCI (even years ago) is not a contraindication. Age >75 is a relative consideration but not a contraindication — per StatPearls-STEMI, patients 75 and older should receive half the normal dose rather than being excluded entirely.
references: Akbar H, Mountfort S. Acute ST-Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI). [Updated 2024 Oct 6]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK532281/ [Open Access]

stem: Post-ACS, dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT) should continue for:

choices.feedback: Too short — premature discontinuation increases stent thrombosis risk.
Lifelong aspirin monotherapy, but DAPT is typically limited to 12 months due to bleeding risk.
Stopping DAPT at discharge dramatically increases recurrent events.

Correct! Standard DAPT (aspirin + P2Y12 inhibitor) for 12 months post-ACS, regardless of whether PCI/stent was performed. After 12 months, continue aspirin indefinitely. P2Y12 options: ticagrelor (preferred — reversible, superior to clopidogrel per PLATO trial) or clopidogrel. May shorten to 6 months if high bleeding risk.

explanation: Current AHA/ACC guidelines recommend 12 months of dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT: aspirin + a P2Y12 inhibitor) after ACS, regardless of whether the patient undergoes PCI with stenting or is managed medically. This duration balances the reduced risk of recurrent ischemic events against the increased bleeding risk. P2Y12 inhibitor options include ticagrelor (preferred per 2023 ESC, reversible, given twice daily), prasugrel (irreversible, contraindicated in prior stroke/TIA), or clopidogrel (if others are contraindicated or unaffordable). After 12 months, DAPT may be extended or shortened based on individualized ischemic vs. bleeding risk assessment.
references: Singh A, Museedi AS, Grossman SA. Acute Coronary Syndrome. [Updated 2023 Jul 10]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK459157/ [Open Access]

After 12 months, DAPT may be extended or shortened based on individualized ischemic vs. bleeding risk assessment.

stem: The hallmark EKG finding in atrial fibrillation is:

choices.text: Absent P waves with irregularly irregular R-R intervals
choices.feedback: Correct! No organized P waves (chaotic atrial activity creates fibrillatory baseline) + irregularly irregular ventricular response. These two features together are pathognomonic for A-fib.
A-fib has narrow QRS unless concurrent bundle branch block or aberrant conduction.
Sawtooth (flutter waves) = atrial flutter, not A-fib.
Regular narrow complex = SVT (AVNRT, AVRT). A-fib is IRREGULARLY irregular.

explanation: The hallmark EKG finding of atrial fibrillation is absent P waves with irregularly irregular R-R intervals. The absence of organized atrial activity (P waves) reflects the chaotic, disorganized electrical depolarization of the atria. The resulting ventricular response is irregularly irregular because AV nodal conduction of these chaotic impulses varies beat to beat. Wide QRS complexes suggest ventricular tachycardia or bundle branch block, not AF (AF typically has narrow QRS unless pre-existing conduction disease or WPW). A sawtooth pattern is characteristic of atrial FLUTTER, not fibrillation. Regular narrow-complex tachycardia suggests SVT (AVNRT, AVRT), not AF — regularity is the key distinguishing feature.

stem: A patient with A-fib, HTN, DM, and age 70. CHA₂DS₂-VASc score and management?

choices.feedback: Calculate: HTN(1) + DM(1) + age 65-74(1) = 3, not 2.
Score is 3. Even at high scores, DOACs are preferred over warfarin unless mechanical valve or mitral stenosis.
Correct! HTN = 1, DM = 1, Age 65-74 = 1. Total = 3. Score ≥2 = anticoagulate. DOACs (apixaban preferred — lowest bleeding in ARISTOTLE trial) are first-line over warfarin for non-valvular A-fib.
Score is 3, not 1. Also, aspirin alone is no longer recommended for stroke prevention in A-fib.

explanation: CHA₂DS₂-VASc scoring: C (CHF) = 1, H (HTN) = 1, A₂ (Age ≥75) = 2, D (DM) = 1, S₂ (prior Stroke/TIA) = 2, V (Vascular disease) = 1, A (Age 65-74) = 1, Sc (Sex category female) = 1. This patient: HTN (1) + DM (1) + Age 70 (1, for age 65-74 category) = Score of 3. With a score ≥2 in males (≥3 in females), oral anticoagulation is recommended. DOACs (apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran, edoxaban) are preferred over warfarin for non-valvular AF due to predictable pharmacokinetics, no INR monitoring, fewer drug-food interactions, and comparable or superior efficacy with lower intracranial hemorrhage risk. Aspirin alone is no longer recommended for stroke prevention in AF at any CHA₂DS₂-VASc score per current guidelines.

stem: When is warfarin preferred over DOACs in A-fib?

choices.feedback: Some DOACs (apixaban) can be used with dose adjustment even in severe CKD. Warfarin is an option but not exclusively required.
DOACs are safe and preferred even in elderly patients.

choices.text: Mechanical heart valve or moderate-to-severe mitral stenosis
choices.feedback: Correct! Warfarin is required for 'valvular' A-fib: mechanical prosthetic valves and moderate-to-severe mitral stenosis. DOACs are NOT approved for these — the RE-ALIGN trial (dabigatran in mechanical valves) was stopped early due to increased thromboembolism and bleeding.
DOACs are preferred for paroxysmal A-fib with appropriate CHA₂DS₂-VASc score.

explanation: Warfarin is preferred over DOACs specifically in patients with mechanical heart valves or moderate-to-severe mitral stenosis (rheumatic valvular AF). StatPearls explicitly states that DOACs 'are recommended over warfarin, except for patients with moderate to severe MS with a mechanical heart valve in place.' The RE-ALIGN trial demonstrated that dabigatran was inferior to warfarin in mechanical valve patients (increased thromboembolism and bleeding), leading to its contraindication in this population. Mitral stenosis creates LA enlargement and stasis independent of the mechanisms DOACs target. Renal failure with GFR <30 requires dose adjustment or avoidance of certain DOACs, but warfarin can still be used — however, this is not the PRIMARY indication where warfarin is preferred. The definitive indications remain mechanical valves and moderate-severe MS.

stem: A-fib of unknown duration. Before cardioversion, you must:

choices.text: Anticoagulate 3 weeks OR perform TEE to exclude LA thrombus
choices.feedback: Correct! A-fib ≥48h or unknown: 3 weeks anticoagulation → cardiovert → 4 weeks post-cardioversion anticoagulation. OR: TEE → if no LA appendage thrombus → cardiovert immediately with anticoagulation. Either approach acceptable.

choices.text: Cardioversion is never safe after 48 hours
choices.feedback: Cardioversion is safe after appropriate anticoagulation or TEE clearance.
Unknown duration could mean >48h → thrombus risk. Must anticoagulate or rule out thrombus.

choices.text: Give aspirin for 1 week then cardiovert
choices.feedback: Aspirin is insufficient for stroke prevention around cardioversion.

explanation: Before cardioverting AF of unknown duration (or >48 hours), you must either: (1) anticoagulate therapeutically for at least 3 weeks, then cardiovert, then continue anticoagulation for at least 4 weeks post-cardioversion; OR (2) perform TEE to exclude left atrial thrombus, then cardiovert with concurrent anticoagulation, then continue for at least 4 weeks. StatPearls states: 'If the duration of AF is more than 48 hours or if the time is unknown, start anticoagulation and maintain INR between 2-3 or commence a factor Xa inhibitor for at least three weeks before and at least four weeks after cardioversion.' The 48-hour threshold exists because thrombus formation risk increases with AF duration. Without these precautions, cardioversion carries a 5-7% stroke risk from embolization of pre-formed thrombus or from post-cardioversion atrial stunning. 'Just cardiovert immediately' is dangerous; aspirin alone is insufficient anticoagulation.

stem: Rate control medications for A-fib include all EXCEPT:

choices.feedback: Digoxin is used as adjunct rate control, especially in HF patients.

Correct! Flecainide is a rhythm control drug (Class IC antiarrhythmic) used to restore/maintain sinus rhythm, NOT for rate control. It can paradoxically INCREASE ventricular rate in A-fib by organizing atrial activity into flutter (must co-administer with AV nodal blocker). Rate control agents: BBs, non-DHP CCBs, digoxin.
Beta-blockers are first-line rate control agents.
Non-DHP CCBs are effective rate control agents (avoid in HFrEF).

explanation: Flecainide is a class IC antiarrhythmic used for RHYTHM control (converting AF to sinus rhythm), NOT for rate control. It works by blocking sodium channels and slowing conduction through the atria, which can terminate AF. It does NOT slow the ventricular rate during ongoing AF and can actually be dangerous — flecainide can organize AF into atrial flutter with 1:1 conduction, paradoxically INCREASING the ventricular rate. Rate control agents work by slowing conduction through the AV node to reduce ventricular response during AF: beta-blockers (metoprolol) block sympathetic drive to the AV node; non-dihydropyridine CCBs (diltiazem, verapamil) slow AV nodal conduction directly; digoxin increases vagal tone at the AV node. The distinction between rate control (controlling ventricular rate during AF) and rhythm control (converting to/maintaining sinus rhythm) is fundamental to AF management.

stem: Most common cause of atrial fibrillation?

choices.feedback: Thyrotoxicosis is an important and reversible cause but not the most common.
Mitral stenosis is a classic cause but not the most common overall.

Correct! HTN is the #1 cause of A-fib due to left atrial enlargement from chronic pressure overload. Other important causes: valvular disease (especially mitral), HF, thyrotoxicosis (always check TSH in new A-fib), alcohol, PE, post-cardiac surgery, OSA.
'Holiday heart' is a well-known cause but less common than HTN.

explanation: Hypertension is the most common cause of atrial fibrillation, owing to its high prevalence and its direct pathophysiological effects on the atria. Chronic hypertension causes left ventricular hypertrophy, which increases left atrial pressure, leading to left atrial enlargement and fibrosis — the substrate for AF. StatPearls lists hypertension prominently among AF etiologies and notes that 'advanced age, high blood pressure, underlying heart and lung disease' are key risk factors. While thyrotoxicosis, mitral stenosis, and alcohol use are all well-recognized causes of AF, they are far less prevalent in the population than hypertension. Mitral stenosis is a classic association (especially rheumatic heart disease) but relatively uncommon in developed countries. Thyrotoxicosis should always be screened for in new-onset AF but accounts for a small minority of cases. Alcohol use ('holiday heart syndrome') is a recognized trigger but not the most common underlying cause.
references: Nesheiwat Z, Goyal A, Jagtap M. Atrial Fibrillation. [Updated 2023 Apr 26]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK526072/ [Open Access]

stem: EAST-AFNET 4 trial showed that early rhythm control:

choices.text: Has no benefit over rate control
choices.feedback: That was the AFFIRM trial conclusion. EAST-AFNET 4 challenged this.

choices.text: Should only be used in patients under 50
choices.feedback: The trial included patients of various ages and showed benefit across subgroups.

choices.text: Reduces cardiovascular outcomes when initiated within 1 year of diagnosis
choices.feedback: Correct! EAST-AFNET 4 (2020): early rhythm control (within 1 year of A-fib diagnosis) reduced composite of CV death, stroke, and HF hospitalization by 21%. This shifted practice toward earlier rhythm control, especially with catheter ablation becoming safer and more effective.
Anticoagulation decisions are based on CHA₂DS₂-VASc score regardless of rhythm control strategy.

explanation: The EAST-AFNET 4 trial (Kirchhof et al., NEJM 2020) demonstrated that early rhythm control — initiated within 1 year of AF diagnosis — reduced the composite of cardiovascular death, stroke, and hospitalization for heart failure or ACS compared to usual care (rate control with escalation only if symptomatic). This was a landmark shift because prior trials (AFFIRM, RACE) had shown no survival advantage of rhythm control — but those enrolled patients with longstanding AF and used older antiarrhythmics. EAST-AFNET 4 specifically targeted EARLY intervention. Key caveats: early rhythm control does NOT eliminate the need for anticoagulation — stroke prevention based on CHA₂DS₂-VASc score must continue regardless of rhythm strategy. The benefit was not limited to patients under 50; the trial enrolled patients across a wide age range with recent-onset AF and cardiovascular risk factors.

stem: Digoxin in A-fib rate control — key limitation?

choices.feedback: Digoxin is available in both oral and IV formulations.

choices.text: It only controls resting heart rate, not exercise rate
choices.feedback: Correct! Digoxin controls resting HR via vagal enhancement but does NOT control exercise HR (sympathetic activation overrides vagal tone). This is why it's used as an adjunct, not first-line. Best role: HFrEF patients with A-fib (inotropic + rate control). Narrow therapeutic window — monitor for toxicity (visual changes, nausea, arrhythmias).

choices.text: It converts A-fib to sinus rhythm
choices.feedback: Digoxin does NOT cardiovert — it's purely for rate control.
Bradycardia is a side effect but not the key limitation.

explanation: Digoxin's key limitation in AF rate control is that it only controls resting heart rate, not exercise heart rate. Digoxin works by increasing vagal tone at the AV node (parasympathetic effect), which slows conduction during rest. However, during exercise, sympathetic activation overwhelms digoxin's vagal effect, allowing the ventricular rate to increase unchecked. This makes digoxin inadequate as monotherapy for rate control in active patients. StatPearls notes digoxin 'is not advised as a first-line agent pertaining to its adverse effects and tolerance.' Other limitations include its narrow therapeutic index (toxicity risk with hypokalemia, renal impairment), and observational data suggesting possible increased mortality. Digoxin is available in both oral and IV forms (so that distractor is wrong). It does NOT convert AF to sinus rhythm — it is strictly a rate control agent. While bradycardia can occur with toxicity, the primary clinical limitation is inadequate exercise rate control.

stem: Stanford Type A aortic dissection involves the:

choices.feedback: Isolated abdominal aortic dissection is extremely rare.

choices.text: Aortic arch only, sparing the ascending
choices.feedback: Arch involvement with ascending = Type A.

Correct! Type A = any involvement of ascending aorta (regardless of descending extension). Surgical emergency — mortality 1-2% per hour without surgery. Type B = descending only (distal to left subclavian) — medical management unless complicated.
Descending only = Type B.

explanation: Stanford Type A aortic dissection involves the ascending aorta, regardless of the site of the primary intimal tear. It is defined as a dissection proximal to the brachiocephalic artery. Stanford Type B originates distal to the left subclavian artery and involves only the descending aorta. The DeBakey system further subdivides: Type 1 originates in the ascending aorta and extends to the descending aorta, Type 2 is limited to the ascending aorta, and Type 3 begins in the descending aorta. Type A dissections are nearly twice as common as Type B and carry higher mortality without urgent surgical intervention. The most common site for the intimal tear is approximately 2 to 2.5 cm above the aortic root.

stem: First-line antihypertensive for aortic dissection?

choices.feedback: Too slow onset for acute dissection emergency.
Hydralazine causes reflex tachycardia — worsens dissection.
Nitroprusside causes reflex tachycardia → increased shear stress. Must give BB first.

Correct! IV BB first: reduces HR (target <60) and BP (SBP 100-120). Esmolol (titratable, short-acting) or labetalol (alpha + beta). BB BEFORE any vasodilator to prevent reflex tachycardia. Then add nicardipine or nitroprusside if needed.

explanation: IV beta-blockers are the first-line treatment for acute aortic dissection. The goal is to reduce both heart rate and blood pressure — decreasing aortic wall shear stress (dP/dt). Beta-blockers reduce the force and velocity of left ventricular contraction, which is the primary driver of dissection propagation. Target heart rate is less than 60 bpm and systolic blood pressure between 100 and 120 mmHg. Esmolol (short-acting, titratable) and labetalol (combined alpha/beta blockade) are commonly used. Vasodilators like nitroprusside must never be given before adequate beta-blockade because they cause reflex tachycardia that increases shear stress.

This is not a pharmacokinetic interaction.

choices.text: Vasodilators alone cause reflex tachycardia → increased aortic shear stress
choices.feedback: Correct! Vasodilators (nitroprusside, hydralazine) lower BP but trigger baroreceptor-mediated reflex tachycardia → increased dP/dt (rate of aortic pressure rise) → more wall stress → propagates dissection. BB first eliminates reflex tachycardia before adding vasodilators.

choices.text: Vasodilators cause coronary steal in dissection
choices.feedback: Coronary steal is not the primary concern here.

explanation: Vasodilators (nitroprusside, hydralazine, nicardipine without beta-blockade) lower systemic vascular resistance and blood pressure, triggering a baroreceptor-mediated reflex tachycardia. The increased heart rate and left ventricular contractility (dP/dt) dramatically increase shear stress on the aortic wall, which can propagate the dissection flap. Beta-blockers must be given first to blunt this reflex before adding any vasodilator. The concept of 'anti-impulse therapy' targets reducing both the rate of rise of aortic pressure (dP/dt) and the absolute blood pressure. This is why labetalol is particularly useful — it provides both alpha and beta blockade simultaneously.

stem: Imaging gold standard for diagnosing aortic dissection?

choices.feedback: CXR shows widened mediastinum in 60-90% but is insufficiently sensitive to confirm or exclude.

Correct! CTA: >95% sensitivity and specificity. Shows intimal flap separating true and false lumens, extent of dissection, branch vessel involvement. Fast and widely available. TEE is the best bedside test for unstable patients who cannot go to CT.
Has no role in aortic dissection diagnosis.
TTE has limited sensitivity for dissection. TEE is much better.

explanation: CT angiography (CTA) is the first-line imaging modality for diagnosing aortic dissection due to its high sensitivity and specificity, widespread availability, and rapid acquisition time. CTA clearly shows the intimal flap separating true and false lumens, aortic dilation, intramural hematoma, contrast leak (indicating rupture), and branch vessel involvement. It effectively differentiates Type A from Type B dissections. Transesophageal echocardiography (TEE) is preferred in hemodynamically unstable patients or when CTA is unavailable — it provides real-time assessment of the ascending aorta, aortic regurgitation, and cardiac tamponade. MRI is highly accurate but impractical in acute settings. Chest X-ray may show widened mediastinum (>8 cm) but up to 20% of patients have normal films, so a normal CXR does not exclude dissection.

stem: Aortic dissection can mimic STEMI when it involves:

choices.feedback: Correct! Type A dissection can extend into the right coronary ostium → RCA occlusion → inferior STEMI. This is catastrophic if misdiagnosed as primary ACS and treated with fibrinolytics (causes fatal hemorrhage into the dissection). Always consider dissection in chest pain with aortic regurgitation, pulse deficits, or tearing back pain.
Brachiocephalic involvement causes stroke symptoms, not STEMI.
Descending dissection does not typically involve coronary arteries.
Renal involvement causes renal malperfusion (AKI), not STEMI.

explanation: Type A aortic dissection can extend into the coronary artery ostia (most commonly the right coronary artery due to its anterior position), causing acute coronary occlusion that mimics ST-elevation myocardial infarction. ECG findings consistent with acute MI occur in approximately 8% of aortic dissection cases. This is a critical diagnostic trap — administering thrombolytics or anticoagulation for a presumed STEMI in the setting of unrecognized aortic dissection is catastrophic. The inferior leads are most commonly affected (RCA territory). Clinical clues that suggest dissection rather than primary MI include sudden tearing pain maximal at onset, blood pressure discrepancy between arms, pulse deficits, widened mediastinum on CXR, and new aortic regurgitation murmur.

stem: A patient with Marfan syndrome presents with sudden tearing chest pain. Which risk factor predisposes them?

choices.text: Cystic medial necrosis from fibrillin-1 deficiency
choices.feedback: Correct! Marfan syndrome = fibrillin-1 (FBN1) gene mutation → defective elastic tissue → cystic medial necrosis → weakened aortic wall → dissection and aneurysm. Other connective tissue disorders: Ehlers-Danlos (type IV — vascular), Loeys-Dietz, Turner syndrome. These patients need lifelong aortic surveillance.
Calcification is degenerative, not the mechanism in Marfan.
Atherosclerosis is less relevant in Marfan — the connective tissue defect is key.
Marfan affects connective tissue structure, not coagulation.

explanation: Marfan syndrome results from mutations in the FBN1 gene encoding fibrillin-1, a glycoprotein essential for elastic fiber formation in the aortic media. Defective fibrillin-1 leads to cystic medial necrosis (degeneration of the elastic and smooth muscle layers of the aortic wall), dramatically weakening the vessel and predisposing to dissection and aneurysm formation. Per IRAD data, Marfan syndrome was present in 50% of aortic dissection patients younger than 40, compared to only 2% of older patients. Other genetic conditions with similar aortic wall pathology include Ehlers-Danlos syndrome (vascular type), Loeys-Dietz syndrome, Turner syndrome, and bicuspid aortic valve. These connective tissue disorders cause structural abnormalities in the elastic fiber meshwork, predisposing to aneurysm and dissection.

stem: Type B dissection is treated medically UNLESS which complication occurs?

choices.feedback: Correct! Uncomplicated Type B: medical therapy (anti-impulse). Complicated Type B requires endovascular repair (TEVAR): organ malperfusion (renal failure, mesenteric ischemia, limb ischemia), aortic rupture, refractory pain despite optimal medical therapy, rapid aortic expansion.
Mild HTN is expected and managed with anti-impulse therapy.
Persistent pain may indicate complication but isolated pain is managed medically with reassessment.
Age alone does not change the medical management strategy for uncomplicated Type B.

explanation: Uncomplicated Stanford Type B aortic dissection is managed medically with anti-impulse therapy (beta-blockers to reduce heart rate and blood pressure). Surgical or endovascular intervention becomes necessary when complications develop: malperfusion syndromes (renal ischemia causing AKI, mesenteric ischemia causing bowel necrosis, limb ischemia from iliac/femoral obstruction), aortic rupture or contained rupture, rapid aortic expansion, refractory pain despite medical therapy, or refractory hypertension. Thoracic endovascular aortic repair (TEVAR) has become the preferred intervention for complicated Type B dissections, covering the primary entry tear to redirect flow into the true lumen and promote false lumen thrombosis.

stem: Chest pain that is maximal at onset with tearing quality suggests dissection over MI because:

choices.feedback: MI pain can be severe but typically crescendos over minutes.

Correct! Dissection: sudden maximal pain from the moment of intimal tear — patients can tell you the exact second. MI: gradual buildup as ischemia progresses. Dissection radiates to the back (interscapular), MI to jaw/arm. This temporal distinction is high-yield for distinguishing the two.

choices.text: MI always presents with shortness of breath first
choices.feedback: MI presents with chest pain/pressure as the primary symptom.
Dissection CAN cause EKG changes (coronary involvement → STEMI, pericardial effusion → low voltage).

explanation: The hallmark distinguishing feature of aortic dissection pain is that it is maximal at onset — patients can often pinpoint the exact moment it began. This reflects the sudden mechanical event of the intimal tear. In contrast, myocardial infarction pain typically crescendos gradually over minutes as ischemia progresses. StatPearls identifies pain intensity at onset as one of the most reliable historical factors in diagnosing acute aortic dissection. The classic description is sudden, severe, 'tearing' or 'ripping' chest or back pain. Anterior chest pain suggests ascending aorta involvement (Type A), while interscapular back pain suggests descending involvement (Type B). The pain may migrate as the dissection propagates distally. Notably, approximately 10% of patients (especially those with Marfan syndrome) present without pain, complicating diagnosis.

stem: The 300 method for calculating heart rate uses:

choices.feedback: The 300 method is an independent EKG-based calculation.
The 300 method divides, not multiplies.
PR interval is used for assessing heart block, not heart rate.

choices.text: 300 ÷ number of large boxes between R waves
choices.feedback: Correct! Count large boxes between two consecutive R waves → 300 ÷ boxes = HR. Quick markers: 1 box = 300, 2 = 150, 3 = 100, 4 = 75, 5 = 60, 6 = 50. Only accurate for regular rhythms. For irregular rhythms: count R waves in a 6-second strip × 10.

explanation: The 300 method calculates heart rate by dividing 300 by the number of large boxes (each = 0.2 seconds) between two consecutive R waves. For example, 2 large boxes = 150 bpm, 3 = 100, 4 = 75, 5 = 60, 6 = 50. This works for regular rhythms. For irregular rhythms, count R waves in 6 seconds (30 large boxes) and multiply by 10. The method works because 300 large boxes = 60 seconds, so 300 ÷ R-R interval in large boxes = beats per minute.

stem: Diffuse ST elevation with PR depression and no reciprocal changes suggests:

choices.feedback: Hyperkalemia shows peaked T waves, widened QRS, and eventually sine wave — not diffuse ST elevation.
Brugada shows coved ST elevation in V1-V3 only, not diffuse.
STEMI has territorial ST elevation (corresponding to a coronary artery) WITH reciprocal ST depression in other leads.

Correct! Pericarditis: diffuse concave-up (smiley face) ST elevation in multiple non-contiguous leads, PR depression (most specific finding, best in II), NO reciprocal changes. STEMI: convex-up ST elevation in contiguous leads with reciprocal depression. This distinction is high-yield.

explanation: Diffuse ST elevation with PR depression (PR segment depression in most leads, PR elevation in aVR) in the absence of reciprocal ST depression is the classic ECG pattern of acute pericarditis. STEMI shows ST elevation in a coronary territory distribution with reciprocal ST depression in contiguous leads. Hyperkalemia shows peaked T waves, widened QRS, and eventually sine wave pattern. Brugada syndrome shows coved ST elevation in V1-V3 with right bundle branch block pattern. The four stages of pericarditis ECG changes should be recognized.

stem: WPW (Wolff-Parkinson-White) is characterized by:

choices.feedback: WPW has a SHORT PR interval (the accessory pathway bypasses the AV node delay).
Sawtooth = atrial flutter.

choices.text: Short PR + delta wave + wide QRS
choices.feedback: Correct! WPW: accessory pathway (Bundle of Kent) bypasses the AV node → early ventricular depolarization. Short PR (<120 ms), delta wave (slurred QRS upstroke), wide QRS. Danger: if A-fib develops, rapid conduction through accessory pathway → VF. Treatment of A-fib in WPW: procainamide or cardioversion. AVOID AV nodal blockers (digoxin, verapamil, adenosine).

choices.text: Peaked T waves + absent P waves
choices.feedback: Peaked T waves = hyperkalemia. Absent P waves = A-fib.

explanation: Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) syndrome is characterized by a short PR interval (<120 ms, reflecting early ventricular activation via accessory pathway bypassing the AV node), delta wave (slurred upstroke of the QRS from pre-excitation), and wide QRS complex (>100 ms from fusion of normal and accessory pathway conduction). WPW carries risk of re-entrant tachycardia and, critically, rapid ventricular response in atrial fibrillation via the accessory pathway. Prolonged PR suggests AV block. Sawtooth pattern describes atrial flutter. Peaked T waves suggest hyperkalemia.

stem: New LBBB with chest pain should be treated as:

choices.feedback: LBBB is a conduction abnormality, not an arrhythmia.
PE has different EKG findings (S1Q3T3, right heart strain).

Correct! New LBBB in the setting of ischemic symptoms is considered a STEMI equivalent because LBBB obscures ST-segment analysis. These patients should receive emergent reperfusion therapy (PCI). Old/chronic LBBB does not warrant emergent PCI.
New LBBB + chest pain is potentially a STEMI equivalent and should not be observed.

explanation: New left bundle branch block (LBBB) in the setting of chest pain is treated as a STEMI equivalent requiring emergent percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI). New LBBB can mask ST-segment changes of acute MI, so its new appearance with ischemic symptoms warrants emergent catheterization. Sgarbossa criteria can help identify STEMI in the presence of LBBB but should not delay treatment when clinical suspicion is high. This is not atrial fibrillation, pulmonary embolism, or a benign finding — new LBBB with chest pain is an emergency.

stem: Torsades de pointes treatment:

choices.feedback: Correct! IV magnesium sulfate 2g IV is first-line for torsades de pointes. It stabilizes cardiac membrane and suppresses early afterdepolarizations. For refractory cases: overdrive pacing (increases HR → shortens QT). Also correct underlying cause (stop QT-prolonging drugs, replace K+/Mg²+). AVOID amiodarone, procainamide, sotalol — all prolong QT.
Amiodarone PROLONGS QT and can WORSEN torsades. Absolutely contraindicated.
If unstable/pulseless, defibrillate. But first-line pharmacotherapy is magnesium. Synchronized cardioversion is for stable re-entrant SVTs.
Adenosine is for SVT, not ventricular arrhythmias.

explanation: IV magnesium sulfate (1-2g IV over 5-20 minutes) is the first-line treatment for Torsades de Pointes (TdP), a polymorphic ventricular tachycardia occurring in the setting of prolonged QT interval. Magnesium stabilizes cardiac membrane and shortens the QT interval. For unstable TdP, immediate defibrillation is needed. Isoproterenol or temporary overdrive pacing can be used for refractory cases by increasing heart rate and shortening QT. Amiodarone should be AVOIDED — it prolongs QT and can worsen TdP. Adenosine is used for SVT, not ventricular tachycardia.

stem: First EKG change in hyperkalemia?

choices.feedback: Delta waves indicate WPW, not hyperkalemia.
ST elevation suggests ischemia or pericarditis, not hyperkalemia.
Wide QRS occurs later in the progression. The earliest change is different.

Correct! Hyperkalemia EKG progression: peaked T waves (earliest, K+ ~5.5-6.5) → PR prolongation → P wave flattening/loss → QRS widening → sine wave → VF/asystole. First intervention: IV calcium gluconate (cardioprotective — stabilizes membrane). Does NOT lower K+; add insulin/glucose and other shifting agents.

explanation: Peaked, narrow, tall T waves are the earliest ECG change in hyperkalemia (K+ ~5.5-6.5 mEq/L). As potassium rises further: PR prolongation and P wave flattening (6.5-7.5), QRS widening (7.0-8.0), sine wave pattern (>8.0), and eventually ventricular fibrillation/asystole. Delta waves are seen in WPW syndrome. ST elevation occurs in MI or pericarditis. QRS widening occurs with more severe hyperkalemia but is not the first change.

stem: MaRRoW/WiLLiaM mnemonics help identify:

choices.feedback: Correct! MaRRoW = RBBB: M-shaped (rsR') in right precordial leads (V1-V2), W-shaped (wide S) in lateral leads (I, V6). WiLLiaM = LBBB: W-shaped in V1, M-shaped (broad notched R) in lateral leads (I, aVL, V5-V6). QRS must be >120 ms for either diagnosis.
Heart blocks are classified by PR interval and P-QRS relationship.
Flutter has sawtooth pattern; A-fib has absent P waves with irregular R-R.
ST patterns relate to ischemia/infarction, not bundle branch blocks.

explanation: MaRRoW and WiLLiaM are mnemonics for distinguishing right and left bundle branch blocks in leads V1 and V6. MaRRoW = in RBBB, V1 shows M-shaped rsR' pattern (two peaks) and V6 shows W-shaped broad S wave. WiLLiaM = in LBBB, V1 shows W-shaped QS/rS pattern (deep S wave) and V6 shows M-shaped broad R wave with notched peak. These mnemonics do not identify heart block types, atrial flutter/fibrillation, or ST elevation patterns.

stem: A-fib with WPW should NOT be treated with which drug?

choices.feedback: Cardioversion is safe and appropriate if the patient is hemodynamically unstable.
Procainamide is actually one of the preferred treatments — it slows accessory pathway conduction.

Correct! In A-fib with WPW, blocking the AV node forces ALL conduction through the accessory pathway → extremely rapid ventricular response → VF → cardiac arrest. NEVER give digoxin, verapamil, diltiazem, or adenosine. Safe options: procainamide (slows accessory pathway), cardioversion (if unstable).
Amiodarone can be considered cautiously but AV nodal blockers are the critical contraindication.

explanation: In atrial fibrillation with WPW, AV nodal blocking agents (adenosine, beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers, digoxin) are strictly contraindicated. Blocking the AV node removes the 'braking' effect on ventricular rate, forcing all conduction down the unregulated accessory pathway, which can precipitate ventricular fibrillation and cardiac arrest. Electrical cardioversion is the treatment of choice for unstable patients. Procainamide is safe (slows accessory pathway conduction). IV amiodarone is also considered acceptable by some guidelines, though procainamide is preferred.

stem: A patient has EF 30%, dilated LV, and S3 gallop. Classification?

choices.feedback: Cor pulmonale = right heart failure from pulmonary disease, not primary LV dilation.
Restrictive = stiff ventricle with preserved size but impaired filling (diastolic).
HFpEF has EF ≥50% with stiff/non-compliant ventricle. EF 30% is reduced.
Correct! EF ≤40% = HFrEF. Dilated LV with impaired contraction. S3 gallop from rapid ventricular filling (volume overload). This patient needs GDMT: ARNI + BB + MRA + SGLT2i.

explanation: An EF of 30% meets the definition of HFrEF (LVEF ≤40%) per the 2022 AHA/ACC/HFSA guidelines. The dilated LV and S3 gallop are hallmarks of systolic dysfunction: the ventricle enlarges and weakens, leading to increased end-diastolic volume and rapid early filling that produces the S3 sound. This contrasts with HFpEF (LVEF ≥50%), where the ventricle is stiff rather than dilated and produces an S4 gallop from atrial contraction against a noncompliant ventricle. Restrictive cardiomyopathy shows normal or near-normal chamber size with severely impaired diastolic filling. Cor pulmonale is right-sided heart failure from pulmonary disease, not left ventricular systolic dysfunction. The current classification also recognizes HFmrEF (LVEF 41-49%) and HF with improved EF (previously ≤40%, now >40%).

stem: Which diuretic REDUCES MORTALITY in HFrEF?

choices.feedback: Correct! Spironolactone (MRA) reduces mortality by ~30% in HFrEF (RALES trial). It's an aldosterone antagonist with anti-fibrotic and anti-remodeling effects beyond diuresis. Monitor for hyperkalemia. Eplerenone is an alternative with fewer anti-androgenic side effects.
Loop diuretics relieve congestion symptoms but have NO mortality benefit.
Metolazone augments loop diuretic effect but does not reduce mortality.
HCTZ is a thiazide diuretic used for HTN; no mortality benefit in HF.

explanation: Spironolactone (an MRA/mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist) is the only option listed that reduces mortality in HFrEF. The RALES trial demonstrated a 30% mortality reduction with spironolactone in NYHA class III-IV HFrEF. MRAs are now one of the 'four pillars' of guideline-directed medical therapy for HFrEF (along with beta-blockers, ARNI/ACEi/ARB, and SGLT2 inhibitors). In contrast, loop diuretics (furosemide) relieve congestion and improve symptoms but have never shown mortality benefit — they are essential for volume management but are not disease-modifying. Metolazone and hydrochlorothiazide are thiazide diuretics used as adjuncts when patients are resistant to loop diuretics, also without mortality benefit. MRA prescribing requires monitoring: eGFR >30, potassium <5.0.

MRAs are now one of the 'four pillars' of guideline-directed medical therapy for HFrEF (along with beta-blockers, ARNI/ACEi/ARB, and SGLT2 inhibitors). In contrast, loop diuretics (furosemide) relieve congestion and improve symptoms but have never shown mortality benefit — they are essential for volume management but are not disease-modifying. Metolazone and hydrochlorothiazide are thiazide diuretics used as adjuncts when patients are resistant to loop diuretics, also without mortality benefit. MRA prescribing requires monitoring: eGFR >30, potassium <5.0.

stem: Only three beta-blockers are proven to reduce mortality in HFrEF. They are:

choices.feedback: Correct! ONLY these 3 BBs are evidence-based for HFrEF mortality reduction: carvedilol (COPERNICUS), metoprolol succinate (MERIT-HF), bisoprolol (CIBIS-II). Metoprolol tartrate is NOT the same — only the succinate (extended-release) formulation is proven.
Metoprolol tartrate (immediate-release) is NOT proven. Sotalol is primarily antiarrhythmic.
HF mortality benefit is specific to these 3 agents only.
None of these three have been proven in HFrEF mortality trials.

explanation: Only three beta-blockers have RCT evidence for mortality reduction in HFrEF: carvedilol (COPERNICUS, CAPRICORN trials), metoprolol succinate (MERIT-HF trial), and bisoprolol (CIBIS-II trial). This is not a class effect — other beta-blockers have not shown mortality benefit and some may be harmful. Critical distinction: metoprolol succinate (extended-release) has the evidence, NOT metoprolol tartrate (immediate-release), which was used in the COMET trial and was inferior to carvedilol. Sotalol has antiarrhythmic properties but no HF mortality benefit. Atenolol, propranolol, and nadolol lack HFrEF outcome data. Beta-blockers should be started at low doses and titrated to target doses as tolerated, and should not be initiated during acute decompensation.

stem: SGLT2 inhibitors in HFrEF benefit which patients?

choices.feedback: Correct! DAPA-HF (dapagliflozin) and EMPEROR-Reduced (empagliflozin) showed mortality and HF hospitalization reduction in HFrEF patients WITH and WITHOUT diabetes. SGLT2i are now a standard pillar of HF therapy. Mechanism includes natriuresis, improved cardiac energetics, and reduced fibrosis.
CKD patients benefit from SGLT2i, but the HF benefit extends to patients with normal kidney function.
Benefit seen across NYHA classes II-IV.
A common misconception. SGLT2i benefit HFrEF patients regardless of diabetes status.

explanation: SGLT2 inhibitors (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin) carry a Class 1A recommendation for HFrEF regardless of diabetes status. The DAPA-HF trial (dapagliflozin) and EMPEROR-Reduced trial (empagliflozin) both demonstrated reduced cardiovascular death and HF hospitalization in patients with and without diabetes. SGLT2 inhibitors are now considered one of the four pillars of HFrEF GDMT (alongside beta-blockers, ARNI/ACEi/ARB, and MRAs). They have a Class 2A recommendation for HFmrEF and HFpEF as well. Their mechanism in HF extends beyond glucose lowering — they promote natriuresis, reduce preload, improve cardiac energetics, and decrease myocardial fibrosis. Contraindications include symptomatic hypotension, SBP <95 mmHg, and eGFR <30.

stem: S4 gallop is caused by:

choices.text: Atrial contraction against a stiff ventricle
choices.feedback: Correct! S4 = late diastolic sound from forceful atrial contraction pushing blood into a non-compliant ventricle. Causes: HTN (LVH), HCM, aortic stenosis, acute MI. S4 disappears in A-fib (no organized atrial kick). S4 can be normal in elderly.
That describes S3. S3 = passive rapid filling in early diastole.
MVP causes a mid-systolic click, not S4.
VSD causes a holosystolic murmur, not a gallop.

explanation: The S4 gallop (atrial gallop) is a low-frequency sound occurring in late diastole, just before S1, produced by forceful atrial contraction against a stiff, noncompliant ventricle. It is heard best with the bell of the stethoscope at the apex. S4 is characteristic of diastolic dysfunction (HFpEF) — conditions like hypertensive heart disease, hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, and aortic stenosis that cause ventricular hypertrophy and decreased compliance. This contrasts with the S3 gallop (ventricular gallop), which occurs in early diastole from rapid filling of a volume-overloaded, dilated ventricle — characteristic of systolic dysfunction (HFrEF). S4 is absent in atrial fibrillation (no organized atrial contraction). MVP produces a mid-systolic click, not a gallop. VSD produces a holosystolic murmur.
references: Shams P, Malik A, Chhabra L. Heart Failure (Congestive Heart Failure). [Updated 2025 Feb 26]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430873/ [Open Access]

stem: BNP may be FALSELY LOW in which condition?

choices.feedback: BNP naturally increases with age (falsely high).
A-fib causes falsely elevated BNP from atrial stretch.

Correct! BNP is falsely LOW in obesity — adipose tissue expresses neprilysin which degrades BNP. Use lower diagnostic threshold in obese patients. NT-proBNP may be more reliable. BNP falsely HIGH in: renal failure, A-fib, PE, older age, sepsis.
Renal failure causes falsely HIGH BNP (reduced clearance).

explanation: Obesity causes falsely low BNP levels because adipose tissue expresses neprilysin, the enzyme that degrades natriuretic peptides (BNP and ANP). This is clinically important: obese patients with genuine heart failure may have BNP levels below typical diagnostic thresholds, leading to missed or delayed diagnosis. Lower BNP cutoffs should be used in obese patients. The distractors all INCREASE BNP: advanced age raises baseline BNP (higher thresholds needed in elderly), atrial fibrillation elevates BNP through atrial stretch and neurohormonal activation, and renal failure elevates BNP due to decreased renal clearance (especially NT-proBNP, which is exclusively renally cleared). Understanding what raises vs. lowers BNP is essential for accurate clinical interpretation.
references: Shams P, Malik A, Chhabra L. Heart Failure (Congestive Heart Failure). [Updated 2025 Feb 26]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430873/ [Open Access]

stem: Acute decompensated HF with pulmonary edema. First-line treatment?

choices.feedback: Do NOT start beta-blockers during acute decompensation — may worsen hemodynamics.

Correct! Acute pulmonary edema: sit upright, O2/BiPAP (NIV preferred over intubation), IV furosemide (diuresis), IV nitroglycerin (preload/afterload reduction). BiPAP reduces work of breathing and preload. If cardiogenic shock (hypotension): inotropes (dobutamine) ± mechanical support.
Fluid bolus worsens pulmonary edema! These patients are volume overloaded.
Cardioversion is for arrhythmia-driven instability, not primary pulmonary edema.

explanation: IV furosemide (a loop diuretic) is the first-line treatment for acute decompensated HF with pulmonary edema. It acts within minutes when given IV, producing rapid diuresis and preload reduction. Initial IV dosing should be at least equivalent to the patient's home oral dose; diuretic-naive patients typically receive 20-IV. Additional therapies include IV nitroglycerin (afterload and preload reduction) and non-invasive positive pressure ventilation (BiPAP/CPAP) for respiratory support. IV metoprolol would be harmful in acute decompensation — beta-blockers are negative inotropes that should not be initiated during active decompensation (they are continued only if the patient was previously on a stable dose). IV normal saline would worsen pulmonary edema. Immediate cardioversion is only indicated for hemodynamically unstable tachyarrhythmias.
references: Golla MSG, Hajouli S, Ludhwani D. Heart Failure and Ejection Fraction. [Updated 2024 May 5]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK553115/ [Open Access]

stem: In HFrEF, sacubitril-valsartan (ARNI) is preferred over ACEi because:

choices.text: It replaces the need for beta-blockers
choices.feedback: ARNI does NOT replace BBs. All four GDMT pillars should be used together.

choices.text: PARADIGM-HF showed superior mortality and HF hospitalization reduction
choices.feedback: Correct! PARADIGM-HF: sacubitril-valsartan reduced CV death and HF hospitalization by 20% vs enalapril. Sacubitril inhibits neprilysin (increases natriuretic peptides → vasodilation, natriuresis). Key safety: 36-hour washout when switching FROM ACEi TO ARNI (risk of angioedema). Never combine ARNI + ACEi.

choices.text: It has fewer side effects than all other HF drugs
choices.feedback: ARNIs can cause hypotension and have the angioedema risk, especially without washout from ACEi.

choices.text: It is cheaper and more widely available
choices.feedback: ARNIs are actually more expensive than ACEi.

explanation: The PARADIGM-HF trial demonstrated that sacubitril-valsartan (ARNI) reduced cardiovascular death and HF hospitalization by 20% compared to enalapril (ACEi) in HFrEF patients. Sacubitril inhibits neprilysin, the enzyme that degrades beneficial natriuretic peptides (BNP, ANP) and bradykinin, while valsartan blocks the RAAS. This dual mechanism provides superior neurohormonal modulation. ARNI does NOT replace beta-blockers — all four pillars of GDMT (ARNI, beta-blocker, MRA, SGLT2i) should be used together. ARNI has a unique safety concern: it must never be combined with ACEi (36-hour washout required) due to severe angioedema risk. ARNI is more expensive than generic ACEi, not cheaper. The PARAGON-HF trial also showed benefit in HFpEF, particularly in women.

stem: What DEFINES a hypertensive emergency vs urgency?

choices.feedback: Treatment resistance doesn't define emergency vs urgency.

Correct! End-organ damage (encephalopathy, ACS, HF, dissection, AKI, eclampsia, retinopathy) is what makes it an EMERGENCY. Without end-organ damage, even very high BP is urgency (treated with oral meds). The BP number alone is insufficient.
The absolute number doesn't define it — a patient at 190/115 WITH stroke is an emergency.
Duration is not the distinguishing factor.

explanation: The defining distinction between hypertensive emergency and urgency is the presence of acute end-organ damage, NOT a specific BP threshold. Hypertensive emergency = severely elevated BP with evidence of acute target-organ injury (encephalopathy, stroke, MI, pulmonary edema, aortic dissection, AKI, eclampsia). Hypertensive urgency = severely elevated BP without end-organ damage. The BP number alone does not determine classification — patients with chronic uncontrolled HTN may tolerate very high pressures without acute injury, while previously normotensive patients (e.g., eclampsia) may develop emergencies at lower BP levels. As StatPearls emphasizes: 'treat the patient, not the number.' Emergency requires IV antihypertensives and ICU admission; urgency requires oral medications and outpatient follow-up.
references: Alley WD, Schick MA. Hypertensive Emergency. [Updated 2023 Jul 24]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470371/ [Open Access]

stem: How fast should BP be lowered in most hypertensive emergencies?

choices.feedback: Too rapid — risks watershed infarcts from cerebral hypoperfusion.

choices.text: Do not lower BP at all for 24 hours
choices.feedback: Ongoing end-organ damage requires prompt (but controlled) BP reduction.

choices.text: Reduce MAP by ≤25% in first hour, then 160/100 over 2-6 hours
choices.feedback: Correct! Gradual reduction prevents hypoperfusion injury. The brain has adapted autoregulatory curves to higher pressures. Exceptions: aortic dissection (aggressive — SBP <120 ASAP), eclampsia (<160 within 30-60 min), ischemic stroke pre-tPA (<185/110).
50% reduction risks ischemic complications from hypoperfusion.

explanation: In most hypertensive emergencies, BP should be reduced gradually: MAP by no more than 25% in the first hour, then to approximately 160/100 mmHg over 2-6 hours, with normalization over 24-48 hours. This stepwise approach prevents iatrogenic hypoperfusion — patients with chronic HTN have rightward-shifted cerebral autoregulation curves, so rapid normalization can cause watershed infarction. The key exception is aortic dissection, which requires more aggressive lowering (SBP <120 within minutes). IV agents must be rapidly titratable — oral agents like clonidine and sublingual nifedipine have no role in acute management because their effects are unpredictable and not easily reversed.

primary.supportingQuote: reduce systolic blood pressure by no more than 25% within the initial hour, followed by a gradual blood pressure reduction to 160/100 mm Hg over the next 2 to 6 hours

supporting.keyFinding: lower the mean arterial pressure by 20% to 25% within the first 1 to 2 hours

The key exception is aortic dissection, which requires more aggressive lowering (SBP <120 within minutes). stem: In hypertensive emergency with aortic dissection, the BP target is:

choices.feedback: That's the general rule. Dissection requires MORE aggressive reduction.

choices.text: SBP <120 mmHg and HR <60 bpm within minutes
choices.feedback: Correct! Dissection is the exception to gradual reduction. Immediate goal: SBP 100-120, HR <60 with IV BB (esmolol/labetalol) to minimize aortic wall shear stress. Add vasodilator only after HR controlled.

choices.text: No specific target — just start oral medications
choices.feedback: Dissection requires IV medications and aggressive targets.
Far too slow for dissection — mortality increases 1-2% per hour.

explanation: Aortic dissection is the critical exception to the gradual 25% MAP reduction rule. The target is SBP <120 mmHg AND HR <60 bpm within 20 minutes using IV beta-blockers (esmolol or labetalol) as first-line. Beta-blockers reduce both BP and the rate of aortic pressure rise (dP/dt), decreasing shear stress on the intimal flap. If beta-blockers alone are insufficient, add IV vasodilators (nicardipine, nitroprusside) AFTER adequate heart rate control — vasodilators alone cause reflex tachycardia, which worsens dissection propagation. The standard 25% MAP reduction would be dangerously inadequate because continued hypertension and tachycardia drive ongoing dissection extension, aortic rupture, and death.

stem: PRES (Posterior Reversible Encephalopathy Syndrome) shows what on MRI?

choices.feedback: PRES primarily affects posterior (parieto-occipital) regions.

Correct! PRES = vasogenic edema from loss of cerebral autoregulation in the posterior circulation (watershed zone). MRI: bilateral parieto-occipital T2/FLAIR hyperintensities. Usually reversible with BP control. Causes: hypertensive emergency, eclampsia, immunosuppressants (tacrolimus, cyclosporine).
Midline shift suggests mass effect (tumor, hemorrhage), not PRES.
Gray matter necrosis suggests ischemic injury, not PRES.

explanation: PRES classically shows bilateral parieto-occipital vasogenic edema on MRI, appearing as hyperintense signals on T2-weighted and FLAIR sequences. The posterior predominance is explained by the sparser sympathetic innervation of the posterior cerebral circulation compared to the anterior (carotid) circulation, making it more vulnerable to autoregulatory breakthrough during acute hypertension. Key MRI features: vasogenic edema (increased ADC, distinguishing from cytotoxic edema/infarction). PRES spares the calcarine cortex, differentiating it from PCA stroke. Atypical regions (frontal lobes, cerebellum, brainstem, basal ganglia) occur in up to 30% of cases. PRES is usually reversible with prompt BP control and treatment of the underlying cause (hypertension, eclampsia, immunosuppressants), but delayed treatment can lead to irreversible infarction.

stem: Which IV antihypertensive is preferred in hypertensive emergency with AKI?

choices.feedback: Nitroprusside is renally cleared and risks cyanide toxicity. Not ideal with AKI.
Hydralazine has unpredictable response and reflex tachycardia — not preferred.
Esmolol is effective for HR/BP but doesn't specifically improve renal perfusion.

Correct! Fenoldopam is a dopamine-1 receptor agonist that lowers BP while improving renal blood flow and GFR. It's the preferred agent when hypertensive emergency is accompanied by acute kidney injury. No cyanide toxicity risk (unlike nitroprusside).

explanation: Fenoldopam is a selective dopamine-1 (D1) receptor agonist that simultaneously lowers BP and increases renal blood flow, GFR, and natriuresis — making it the preferred choice for hypertensive emergency complicated by AKI. Its renal-protective mechanism (D1-mediated renal arteriolar vasodilation) is unique among IV antihypertensives. Nitroprusside should be avoided in renal impairment because thiocyanate (its metabolite) accumulates with reduced renal clearance, causing neurotoxicity. Hydralazine is a direct arteriolar vasodilator but has unpredictable onset and duration, is poorly titratable, and causes reflex tachycardia. Esmolol is an ultra-short-acting beta-blocker used for dissection and perioperative HTN but may reduce renal perfusion and lacks renal-protective properties.

stem: A pregnant patient at 36 weeks with BP 180/110, headache, and proteinuria. Diagnosis and first-line antihypertensive?

choices.feedback: ACE inhibitors are CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy (teratogenic). This is also an emergency, not urgency.

choices.text: Severe preeclampsia — IV labetalol or hydralazine
choices.feedback: Correct! Severe preeclampsia: BP ≥160/110 + proteinuria + end-organ signs (headache, visual changes). First-line: IV labetalol or IV hydralazine (both safe in pregnancy). Also give IV magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis. Definitive treatment: delivery. ACEi/ARBs absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy.
Eclampsia = preeclampsia + seizures. Treatment is IV magnesium, not diazepam. Also, this patient hasn't seized yet.
This is acute severe preeclampsia requiring emergency management, not routine chronic HTN.

explanation: This presentation — BP ≥160/110, headache, and proteinuria at 36 weeks — defines severe preeclampsia. First-line antihypertensives are IV labetalol or IV hydralazine, both safe in pregnancy. Magnesium sulfate should be started concurrently for seizure prophylaxis (it is NOT an antihypertensive). Critical distractor analysis: ACE inhibitors (lisinopril) are absolutely CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy — they cause fetal renal agenesis, oligohydramnios, and neonatal death. This is NOT eclampsia, which requires seizures (headache alone is insufficient). For eclamptic seizures, magnesium sulfate is first-line, NOT diazepam. Simply continuing home medications ignores the new-onset proteinuria and symptoms indicating preeclampsia. Definitive treatment is delivery, and at ≥37 weeks (or 36 weeks with severe features), delivery should be expedited after maternal stabilization.

stem: Nitroprusside risk with prolonged use?

choices.feedback: Correct! Nitroprusside → cyanide → metabolized to thiocyanate. Prolonged use (>48-72h) or high doses → cyanide accumulation → lactic acidosis, AMS, seizures. Treatment: hydroxocobalamin (cyanide antidote) or sodium thiosulfate. Monitor thiocyanate levels if used >48h.
Nitroprusside doesn't cause permanent renal damage directly.
Nitroprusside is metabolized to cyanide, not a direct hepatotoxin.
Bronchospasm is associated with non-selective beta-blockers, not nitroprusside.

explanation: Sodium nitroprusside contains five cyanide (CN) groups per molecule. During metabolism, cyanide is released and normally converted to thiocyanate by hepatic rhodanese, then excreted renally. With prolonged infusion (>48-72 hours) or high doses (>weight-based dosing), cyanide production exceeds detoxification capacity, causing cyanide toxicity — which inhibits cytochrome c oxidase (complex IV), blocking mitochondrial aerobic metabolism and causing lactic acidosis. Clinical signs include tachyphylaxis (rising BP despite increasing dose), altered mental status, seizures, and cardiovascular collapse. Thiocyanate toxicity (distinct from cyanide) can additionally occur in renal failure. Treatment: sodium thiosulfate + hydroxocobalamin. Due to these risks, nitroprusside has been largely replaced by safer agents (nicardipine, clevidipine).
references: Alley WD, Schick MA. Hypertensive Emergency. [Updated 2023 Jul 24]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470371/ [Open Access]

stem: Sublingual nifedipine should be AVOIDED in hypertensive emergencies because:

choices.text: It causes unpredictable precipitous BP drops → stroke/MI
choices.feedback: Correct! Sublingual/bite-and-swallow nifedipine causes rapid, uncontrolled BP drops that can precipitate cerebral ischemia, MI, or death. Banned from use in hypertensive emergencies. Use IV titratable agents instead for controlled, predictable BP reduction.

choices.text: It is ineffective at lowering BP
choices.feedback: Nifedipine is actually very effective but too unpredictable.

choices.text: It only works for pulmonary hypertension
choices.feedback: Nifedipine works systemically but is too dangerous in this context.
Nifedipine is oral/sublingual. The issue is unpredictable absorption and BP drop.

explanation: Sublingual nifedipine (a short-acting dihydropyridine CCB) is explicitly contraindicated in hypertensive emergencies because it causes rapid, unpredictable, and uncontrollable BP drops that have been associated with stroke, MI, and death. Unlike IV agents, sublingual nifedipine cannot be titrated — once administered, the precipitous vasodilation cannot be reversed. The rapid BP drop can fall below the cerebral autoregulatory range (rightward-shifted in chronically hypertensive patients), causing watershed infarcts. This led to a regulatory advisory against the practice. StatPearls states that 'oral medications, such as clonidine and nifedipine, play no role in the immediate management of a hypertensive emergency.' The key principle: agents for HTN emergency must be both rapidly acting AND easily titratable, which requires IV administration with continuous monitoring.

stem: Most common cause of syncope overall?

choices.feedback: AS is a structural cardiac cause — important but not most common.

Correct! Vasovagal accounts for ~40% of all syncope. Excessive parasympathetic response → bradycardia + vasodilation. Classic prodrome (lightheadedness, warmth, nausea, diaphoresis) + triggers (prolonged standing, emotional stress). Generally benign prognosis.
Arrhythmias are the most DANGEROUS cause but not the most common.
Orthostatic is common (~15%) but second to vasovagal.

explanation: Vasovagal (neurocardiogenic) syncope is the most common cause of syncope overall, accounting for approximately 21-35% of all episodes. It results from an inappropriate autonomic response: a trigger (prolonged standing, pain, emotional stress, heat) activates the Bezold-Jarisch reflex, causing paradoxical bradycardia and vasodilation, leading to transient cerebral hypoperfusion. Typical prodromal symptoms include lightheadedness, warmth, diaphoresis, nausea, and visual dimming. Vasovagal syncope is generally benign but recurrent episodes can significantly impact quality of life.

stem: Exertional syncope in a young athlete should raise concern for:

choices.feedback: While dehydration causes exertional symptoms, exertional syncope in young athletes warrants cardiac evaluation.

Correct! HCM is the #1 cause of sudden cardiac death in young athletes (<35). Asymmetric septal hypertrophy → dynamic LVOT obstruction during exertion. Family hx of sudden death + systolic murmur (increases with Valsalva) = red flags. Echo confirms. ICD for high risk.
PE is rare in young athletes without risk factors.
Seizure is not true syncope and presents differently (post-ictal state, tonic-clonic activity).

explanation: Exertional syncope in a young athlete is a red flag for hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM), the most common cause of sudden cardiac death in young athletes. HCM involves asymmetric septal hypertrophy that causes dynamic left ventricular outflow tract (LVOT) obstruction during exercise — when contractility and heart rate increase. The obstruction worsens with Valsalva (decreased preload) and improves with squatting (increased preload). Other causes of exertional syncope include arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy, anomalous coronary arteries, long QT syndrome, Wolff-Parkinson-White, and commotio cordis.

stem: Orthostatic hypotension is defined as:

choices.feedback: 10 mmHg drop is the DBP threshold, not SBP.
HR >30 increase suggests POTS (postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome), which is related but distinct from orthostatic hypotension.

choices.text: SBP drop ≥20 mmHg or DBP drop ≥10 mmHg within 3 minutes of standing
choices.feedback: Correct! Measured at 0, 1, and 3 minutes after standing from supine. HR increase >20 bpm also suggests volume depletion. Causes: dehydration, medications (diuretics, BBs, alpha-blockers), autonomic neuropathy (diabetes, Parkinson).
Symptoms are important but the diagnosis requires objective BP criteria.

explanation: Orthostatic hypotension is defined as a sustained reduction of SBP ≥20 mmHg or DBP ≥10 mmHg within 3 minutes of standing from a supine position. Common causes include volume depletion (hemorrhage, dehydration), medications (antihypertensives, diuretics, alpha-blockers, tricyclics), and autonomic dysfunction (diabetic neuropathy, Parkinson disease, pure autonomic failure). Initial evaluation includes lying-to-standing vital signs. Management focuses on treating the underlying cause, medication adjustment, increased fluid and salt intake, compression stockings, and midodrine or fludrocortisone for refractory cases.

stem: Which test should ALL patients with syncope receive?

choices.feedback: Correct! EKG is recommended for ALL syncope patients. Can identify: arrhythmia (heart block, VT, SVT), prolonged QT (torsades risk), Brugada pattern, WPW (delta wave), prior MI (Q waves), HCM (LVH). Low cost, non-invasive, high yield. Orthostatic vitals should also be checked in all patients.
Invasive and only indicated for suspected coronary disease, not routine syncope.
EEG is for suspected seizure, not routine syncope evaluation.
CT head is for suspected neurologic causes but is not routine for all syncope.

explanation: Every patient presenting with syncope should receive a 12-lead ECG. It is the single most important initial test because it can identify life-threatening cardiac causes including arrhythmias (Brugada, long QT, WPW, complete heart block, ventricular tachycardia), structural heart disease (HCM pattern, RV strain), and ischemia. While the diagnostic yield of ECG in syncope is only about 5%, the conditions it detects carry high mortality if missed. Additional workup (echocardiography, Holter monitor, tilt table testing, electrophysiology study) is guided by clinical features and ECG findings.

stem: What distinguishes seizure from syncope?

choices.feedback: Age does not reliably distinguish the two.
Both can have brief LOC, though seizures may have longer post-ictal confusion.
Both can have triggers — emotional stress triggers vasovagal syncope AND some seizures.

choices.text: Tongue-biting, incontinence, tonic-clonic movements, post-ictal confusion
choices.feedback: Correct! Seizure: lateral tongue-biting (specific), urinary incontinence, tonic-clonic movements, prolonged post-ictal confusion/drowsiness. Syncope: brief LOC with rapid return to normal, prodrome (lightheadedness, warmth), pallor, may have brief myoclonic jerks (convulsive syncope — does NOT mean seizure).

explanation: Key features distinguishing seizure from syncope include: tongue-biting (lateral tongue is highly specific for seizure), urinary incontinence (can occur in both but more common in seizure), tonic-clonic movements (rhythmic, sustained, occurring during the event — vs brief myoclonic jerks that can occur in syncope), and post-ictal confusion (prolonged disorientation lasting minutes to hours after a seizure, vs rapid recovery to baseline in syncope). Syncope patients may have brief myoclonic jerks from cerebral hypoperfusion but these are arrhythmic and last <15 seconds. Witnessed accounts are crucial for distinguishing the two.

stem: Tilt table testing is most useful for diagnosing:

choices.feedback: Carotid stenosis is evaluated by duplex ultrasound or CTA.
AS is diagnosed by echocardiography, not tilt testing.

Correct! Tilt table test reproduces vasovagal syncope in a controlled setting. Patient is tilted 70° for 20-45 min ± isoproterenol provocation. Positive test: reproduction of symptoms with hypotension and/or bradycardia. Used when history is suggestive but diagnosis uncertain. Sensitivity ~60-70%.
VT is diagnosed by EKG/monitoring and EP study, not tilt testing.

explanation: Tilt table testing is most useful for diagnosing vasovagal syncope when the diagnosis is suspected clinically but cannot be confirmed by history alone. The test reproduces the vasovagal reflex in a controlled setting — the patient is tilted to 60-80 degrees for up to 45 minutes while heart rate and blood pressure are continuously monitored. A positive result (reproduction of syncope with associated hypotension and/or bradycardia) supports the diagnosis. Tilt table testing has moderate sensitivity (~60-70%) and specificity (~90%). It is not useful for cardiac syncope, which requires ECG monitoring, echocardiography, or electrophysiology study.

stem: A patient with syncope, murmur that increases with Valsalva, and family history of sudden death. Most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: MVP has a mid-systolic click. While click moves earlier with Valsalva, MVP rarely causes syncope or sudden death.
AS murmur DECREASES with Valsalva. This murmur increases.

Correct! HCM: systolic murmur that increases with Valsalva/standing (decreased preload → smaller LV → more obstruction). Family hx of sudden death (autosomal dominant). stem: In vasovagal syncope, first-line management includes:

choices.feedback: ICD is for ventricular arrhythmias/sudden death risk, not vasovagal syncope.
Aspirin has no role in vasovagal syncope management.

choices.text: Education, trigger avoidance, hydration, counterpressure maneuvers
choices.feedback: Correct! explanation: First-line management of vasovagal syncope is conservative: patient education about triggers and prodromal recognition, trigger avoidance (prolonged standing, heat, dehydration), adequate hydration and salt intake, and physical counterpressure maneuvers (leg crossing with isometric contraction, hand grip, arm tensing) performed during prodromal symptoms. These maneuvers increase venous return and can abort an impending episode. Pharmacotherapy (midodrine, fludrocortisone) is reserved for refractory cases. Pacemaker implantation is considered only for recurrent vasovagal syncope with documented prolonged asystole (cardioinhibitory type).

stem: A patient is pulseless with the monitor showing a chaotic, irregular waveform with no identifiable QRS complexes. What is the rhythm and initial treatment?

choices.feedback: Asystole is a flat line with no electrical activity. This chaotic irregular waveform is VF, which requires defibrillation.
VT shows wide regular complexes. Chaotic irregular waveform without QRS complexes is VF. Also, pulseless rhythms get defibrillation, not synchronized cardioversion.

Correct! VF: chaotic, irregular electrical activity with no organized QRS complexes = cardiac arrest. choices.feedback: The patient is PULSELESS — this is cardiac arrest, not a rate control situation. AFib with RVR has identifiable narrow complexes despite irregular rhythm.

stem: During a cardiac arrest, the monitor shows a flat line. Before calling asystole, what must you verify?

choices.feedback: Defibrillation is NOT indicated for asystole. You must first verify the rhythm is truly asystole.
Transcutaneous pacing is for symptomatic bradycardia, not asystole in cardiac arrest. Evidence does not support pacing for asystole.

Correct!
